title: A solution to Weaver's \(KS_2\)
speaker: Adam Marcus
abstract: We will outline the proof that gives a positive solution of to Weaver's conjecture \(KS_2\). That is, we will show that any isotropic collection of vectors whose outer products sum to twice the identity can be partitioned into two parts such that each part is a small distance from the identity. The distance will depend on the maximum length of the vectors in the collection but not the dimension (the two requirements necessary for Weaver's reduction to a solution of Kadison Singer).

proof_stages.label: Finite-dimensional KS2 target and technical theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates Kadison-Singer, passes to equivalent finite-dimensional frame-partition formulations, defines the bounded Parseval-frame problem underlying Weaver's KS2, fixes notation, and states the main random-vector theorem that will imply KS2.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-polynomial strategy
proof_stages.summary: The spectral-norm objective is translated into a largest-root problem for characteristic polynomials, and computational observations motivate proving that some assignment is no worse than the expected characteristic polynomial while bounding that expected polynomial.

proof_stages.label: Interlacing-family selection principle
proof_stages.summary: Conditional expected characteristic polynomials are organized in an assignment tree, and common interlacers plus the anchor lemma show that an interlacing family has a leaf whose largest root is at most that of the root expected polynomial.

proof_stages.label: Mixed characteristic polynomials are real-rooted
proof_stages.summary: Using real stable polynomials and Borcea-Branden closure lemmas, the conditional expected characteristic polynomials are identified as mixed characteristic polynomials and proved real-rooted, thereby giving the required interlacing family.

proof_stages.label: Barrier bound for the expected root
proof_stages.summary: The expected mixed characteristic polynomial is expressed as a diagonalization of a multivariate polynomial, and a multivariate logarithmic-derivative barrier argument tracks the zero set under the differential operators to bound the largest root by 1 + 3√ε.

proof_stages.label: Deduction of Weaver's KS2
proof_stages.summary: The interlacing selection result and root bound yield the main technical theorem, and a doubled-vector random construction converts a KS2 instance into that theorem to obtain a two-part partition with explicit universal constants.

examples.name: Orthonormal basis as a Parseval frame and obstruction

The orthonormal basis is the minimal Parseval frame with maximal vector norms and obstructs a two-way almost-Parseval partition.
